revision.comment: Bot: mark 1 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24487645413|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:拝所]]
{{multiple image | align = right | direction = vertical | header = Places of Worship in Japan | header_align = center | header_background = silver | width = 230 | image1 = Daisenryo Kofun haisho-3.jpg | caption1 = {{Anchors|Mozu no Miharano Naka no Misazagi}}{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Mozu no Miharano Naka no Misazagi|1=Emperor Nintoku}} {{nihongo|Place of Worship|Haisho}} <ref group="gm">Place of Worship at Mozu no Miharano Naka no Misazagi（{{googlemap|百舌鳥耳原中陵拝所}}） displayed in red.</ref>/Worshiping the {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Spirit of Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}. "Mozu no Miharano Naka no Misazagi" is the official designation by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} for the {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai, Osaka|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai, Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}}, Osaka Prefecture. The place of worship is set in front of the keyhole-shaped Kofun. This is the "General Place of Worship" that visitors are guided to<ref name="宮内庁_陵図">{{Cite web|url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/kunaicho/koho/goryou/pdf/image.pdf |title=Imperial Tomb Image Guide |format=PDF |work=Official Website |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |accessdate=2019-06-01 }}</ref>, and only authorized personnel may enter beyond this point. | image2 = Showa_Shrine.jpg | caption2 = {{Anchors|Musashino no Misazagi}}{{ill|Musashi Imperial Graveyard|lt=Musashino no Misazagi|ja|武藏野陵|むさしののみささぎ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Imperial Graveyard|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{nihongo|Place of Worship|Haisho}} / Musashino no Misazagi refers to the {{ill|Mausoleum of Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mausoleum of Emperor Showa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武蔵陵墓地|1=Musashi Imperial Graveyard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6940153}}, one of the burial sites at the {{ill|Musashi Imperial Graveyard|en|Musashi Imperial Graveyard|qid=Q6940153|lt=Musashino Imperial Mausoleum|1=Musashi Imperial Graveyard}}<ref group="注">The kanji differences between "武蔵" and "武藏" represent new and old orthography, respectively. "Musashino no Misazagi" is the official designation by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}.</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/124/index.html |title=Emperor Showa's Musashino Mausoleum |work=Imperial Tombs (Official Website) |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |accessdate=2019-06-01 }}</ref>. Worshiping the spirit of Emperor Showa. The place of worship is situated at the front of the upper round part of the {{ill|Upper Round, Lower Square Mound|ja|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Upper Round, Lower Square Mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. | image3 = Chikubusumajinja01s3200.jpg | caption3 = {{Anchors|Tsukubusuma Shrine}}{{ill|Tsukubusuma Shrine|en|Tsukubusuma Shrine|qid=Q11643470|lt=Tsukubusuma Shrine|1=Tsukubusuma Shrine}} on {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|qid=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island|1=Chikubu Island}}, Lake Biwa, featuring the {{nihongo|Hachidai Ryuo Place of Worship|はちだいりゅうおう はいじょ}} <ref name="竜王拝所" group="gm">Hachidai Ryuo Place of Worship at Tsukubusuma Shrine（{{googlemap|都久夫須麻神社八大竜王拝所}}） displayed in red.</ref>/Also known as "Dragon God Place of Worship"<ref group="注">The term "Dragon God" ({{ill|Ryujin|ja|龍神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryujin|1=Dragon god|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1206058}}) refers to various water deities of different origins, while "Hachidai Ryuo" specifically refers to Buddhist deities derived from the ancient Indian {{ill|Nāga|en|Nāga|qid=Q271990|lt=Nāga|1=Nāga}} tradition.</ref>. Worshiping the {{ill|Eight Great Dragon Kings|ja|八大竜王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Great Dragon Kings|qq=}} residing in Lake Biwa. Facing the lake’s expanse, this place of worship does not point to a specific location, but rather the entire lake as the seat of the deity. | image4 = Yamamiya-sengen-jinja iseki-1.JPG | caption4 = {{Anchors|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine}}{{ill|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|en|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|qid=Q11467749|lt=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|1=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine}} {{ill|Ritual Site|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135504575}} (left side)<ref group="gm">Yamamiya Sengen Shrine（{{googlemap|山宮浅間神社}}） displayed in red. The ritual site itself is not marked but can be seen in context with Mount Fuji.</ref>/Ancient place of worship for {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. Worshiping Fuji as a sacred peak, the site was shaped using local lava rocks to create a space dedicated to distant worship. This preserves the primitive Shinto aesthetic. }}
== Places of Worship ==
{{Anchors|General Places of Worship}} {{Anchors|Imperial Mausoleums}} In Japan, a place of worship ({{nihongo|haisho|はいしょ}}) refers to a designated area for veneration in front of sacred sites. Many {{ill|Japanese imperial tombs|en|Japanese imperial tombs|qid=Q17218816|lt=Japanese imperial tombs|1=Japanese imperial tombs}} and {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} include places of worship. At shrines, the {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|qid=Q3125745|lt=Haiden (Shinto)|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} often serves this role, but in some cases, such as {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, both a place of worship and a haiden exist<ref group="gm">Place of Worship at Suwa Taisha（{{googlemap|諏訪大社拝所}}）.</ref>.<ref>https://www.nihongomaster.com/japanese/dictionary/word/62653/haisho-%E9%85%8D%E6%89%80-%E3%81%AF%E3%81%84%E3%81%97%E3%82%87</ref> == Haisho == {{Anchors|Haisho (Haijo)|Haijo|General Haisho in Japan}} {{Anchors|Characteristics (Haijo)|Overview (Haijo)}} In general Japanese terminology, a "Haisho" (はいしょ<ref name="Japanese Dictionary"/>/{{Citation needed|はいじょ|date=April 2024}}) refers to a designated place for worship in front of a sacred area.
Many {{ill|Japanese imperial tombs|en|Japanese imperial tombs|qid=Q17218816|lt=Japanese imperial tombs|1=Japanese imperial tombs}}, Empress' tombs, and designated {{ill|Tombs|ja|陵墓|1=Japanese imperial tombs|lt=Imperial tomb|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tombs|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese imperial tombs|qq=}} (including {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}) feature haisho. In the case of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}}, the {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|qid=Q3125745|lt=Haiden (Shinto)|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} serves a similar function as a larger facility, meaning some shrines lack a dedicated haisho. However, some, like {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, include both<ref group="gm">Haisho at Suwa Taisha（{{googlemap|Suwa Taisha Haisho}}）.</ref>, while others, like the {{ill|Hachidai Ryuo Haisho|ja|八大竜王拝所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachidai Ryuo Haisho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504585|1=Hachidai Ryuo Haisho}} of {{ill|Tsukubusuma Shrine|en|Tsukubusuma Shrine|qid=Q11643470|lt=Tsukubusuma Shrine|1=Tsukubusuma Shrine}} (worshiping the {{ill|Hachidai Ryuo|ja|八大竜王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachidai Ryuo|1=Eight Great Dragon Kings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390736}} in {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|qid=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}}), only feature a haisho<ref name="Hachidai Ryuo Haisho" group="gm" />.<ref group="note">The main deity of Tsukubusuma Shrine has a honden but no haiden, and the Hachidai Ryuo has neither a honden nor a haiden.</ref>
Additionally, a haisho situated far from its worship target is known as a "Remote Worship Place" ({{ill|Haisho|lt=Haisho|lt=Yōhaijo|ja|遥拝所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōhaijo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), designed for worshiping from a distance.
Since the Meiji era, imperial mausoleums and empress' tombs often include different types of haisho: '''General Haisho''' for common worshippers, '''Special Haisho''' for authorized individuals, and '''Imperial Haisho''' (御拝所, Gohai-jo) reserved for the emperor or the emperor and empress.<ref group="宮内庁">{{Cite web|url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/kunaicho/koho/goryou/pdf/image.pdf |title=Imperial Tomb Image Guide |format=PDF |work=Official Website |publisher=Imperial Household Agency |accessdate=2019-06-01 }}</ref>
{{multiple image | align = right | direction = vertical | header = Haisho (Uganju) | header_align = center | header_background = silver | width = 230 | image1 = | caption1 = }}
==Yōhaijo==
The term "遥拝所" (yōhaijo) refers to a "remote worship site" in Japanese culture. It is a designated place where individuals can worship or pay respects to a sacred site, such as a shrine or temple, from a distance. This practice is particularly significant when direct access to the sacred site is challenging due to geographical obstacles or other restrictions.
An example of a yōhaijo is the one associated with the {{ill|Kawaguchi Sengen Shrine|ja|河口浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawaguchi Sengen Shrine|qq=}} near Lake Kawaguchi. This remote worship site allows visitors to honor Mount Fuji, which is considered sacred in Shinto belief, without ascending the mountain itself. The site features a torii gate framing a view of Mount Fuji, providing a symbolic connection for worshippers. <ref>https://huannntravel.com/fujisan-torii-kawaguchiko/</ref>
In summary, a yōhaijo serves as a meaningful location for individuals to engage in worship or reflection directed toward a distant sacred place, maintaining spiritual practices even when physical proximity is not possible.
== Uganju == {{Anchors|Haisho (Uganju)|Uganju|Okinawan Haisho}} A "Haisho" (うがんじゅ, うがん) in the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|琉球諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Islands|1=Ryukyu Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10905515}} and {{ill|Amami Islands|en|Amami Islands|qid=Q453510|lt=Amami Islands|1=Amami Islands}} refers to a site where deities are enshrined and worshiped.
These places are often regarded as sacred areas where deities reside{{r|kb}}, and compared to {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|qid=Q11071176|lt=Utaki|1=Ontake}}, they are smaller in scale and involve fewer individuals in their rituals{{r|kb}}. Some also refer to capes where {{ill|Visiting Deities|ja|来訪神|1=visiting god|fr|lt=Visiting deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Visiting Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiho-shin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30925139}} are believed to have arrived{{r|kb}}.
=== Naming === {{Anchors|Naming (Uganju)}} The standard kanji for "Uganju" is "拝所", but the alternate spelling "御願所" can also be found<ref name="Okinawa Encyclopedia_1">{{Harvnb|Okinawa Encyclopedia|1983|loc=Volume 1, p. 279}}</ref>. In northern {{ill|Okinawa Island|en|Okinawa Island|qid=Q600614|lt=Okinawa Island|1=Okinawa Island}}, "Uganju" is also known as "Ugami," while in the {{ill|Amami Islands|en|Amami Islands|qid=Q453510|lt=Amami Islands|1=Amami Islands}}, it is called "Ugami-yama" (拝み山), representing an older phonetic form{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_1}}. In the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|en|Yaeyama Islands|qid=Q696496|lt=Yaeyama Islands|1=Yaeyama Islands}}, phonetic variations derived from "Ogami" (拝み) include "On" in {{ill|Ishigaki Island|en|Ishigaki Island|qid=Q714339|lt=Ishigaki|1=Ishigaki Island}}, {{ill|Taketomi Island|en|Taketomi Island|qid=Q474560|lt=Taketomi|1=Taketomi Island}}, and {{ill|Iriomote Island|en|Iriomote Island|qid=Q432124|lt=Iriomote|1=Iriomote Island}}; "Wan" in {{ill|Kuroshima|en|Kuroshima|qid=Q1207339|lt=Kuroshima, Okinawa|1=Kuroshima}}, {{ill|Kohama Island|en|Kohama Island|qid=Q702603|lt=Kohama Island|1=Kohama Island}}, and {{ill|Aragusuku Islands|en|Aragusuku Islands|qid=Q702414|lt=Aragusuku|1=Aragusuku Islands}}; and "Wa" in {{ill|Hateruma|en|Hateruma|qid=Q697600|lt=Hateruma Island|1=Hateruma}}<ref name="Okinawa Encyclopedia_1"/>. The word "Utaki" (御嶽) is a standard term{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_1}}, but colloquially, "Ugan," "Mui" (森, meaning "forest"), and "Yama" are also used{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_1}}.
=== Characteristics === {{Anchors|Characteristics (Uganju)|Overview (Uganju)}} Haisho vary in form<ref name="Okinawa Encyclopedia_2">{{Harvnb|Okinawa Encyclopedia|1983|loc=Volume 2, p. 495}}</ref>. Generally, they are located in forests beneath tall trees, sometimes marked by stones (such as coral formations){{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_2}}, while others have concrete structures{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_2}}. They are considered sacred spaces{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_2}}. Even Shinto shrines fall within the definition of haisho{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_2}}. Additionally, communal haisho can be found at various facilities, including elementary, middle, and high schools, though the {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|qid=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|1=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology}} does not classify these as religious institutions{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_2}}. On {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|qid=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island|1=Miyako Island}}, haisho may be dedicated to individual guardian deities{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_2}}, called "Maugan" (まうがん), with each person having a unique worship site{{r|Okinawa Encyclopedia_2}}.
== References ==
<references group="gm" /> <references /><references group="宮内庁"/> <references group="注" /> <references group="note" />
}}
{{translated page|ja|拝所|version=100103727|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
